title: The Heart of Discernment
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: The Heart of Discernment - 1 Thessalonians 5_21-22 - Pastor Tito Lyro.txt
outline: The heart of discernment is the Bible
Most believers practice discernment in some areas but not others
1 Thessalonians 5:21: Test all things; hold fast what is good
1 Thessalonians 5:22: Abstain from every form of evil
The Bible as the standard and tool for all discernment
Applying biblical discernment consistently across all of life
summary: Part of a PM series on discernment, focusing on 1 Thessalonians 5:21-22 as the heart of the matter. The central claim: at the heart of discernment is the Bible. While most Christians exercise discernment in some areas, consistent biblical discernment requires applying Scripture across all of life—testing all things by God's Word, holding fast to what is good, and abstaining from every form of evil.
transcript:
We continue our series on biblical discernment. Today we are going to talk about the heart of discernment. At the heart of discernment is the Bible — that is what we want to see today.

I think it is fair to say that most of us, if not all of us, understand the importance of discernment in some areas and practice discernment in some areas, but not in others. Someone might be very discerning about their finances but not very discerning about their eating. God calls us to be discerning in every area — no area is supposed to be outside of that discernment.

We will get to 1 Thessalonians 5:21, which says *test all things* — that is, practice discernment in all areas. But first, let me remind us of the definition of discernment from last week: *Discernment is the acquired skill of understanding and applying God's Word with the purpose of separating truth from error and right from wrong, and then living according to what is right.* Discernment is the acquired skill of applying the Word of God to separate truth from error in the area of doctrine, and right from wrong in the area of practice, and then living accordingly.

James tells us that we are not to be merely hearers of the Word of God but doers of the Word. As we consider this definition of discernment, we see that discernment and judgment go hand in hand. You cannot practice discernment without practicing judgment. You cannot separate right from wrong. You cannot separate truth from error if you are not judging.

## Does the Bible Forbid Judging?

The Bible includes passages that tell us we are not to judge — at least they seem to say that. If we believe that God is the author of the Bible and that God always and only speaks the truth, we must believe the Bible never contradicts itself. So rather than declaring contradictions, we need to seek to understand and harmonize these passages.

We are going to start with the passage that is probably the best-known passage among unbelievers. They might not know the reference, but they can quote it: *"Judge not."* Turn to Matthew chapter 7. This is our Lord teaching.

> *"Judge not, that you be not judged. For with what judgment you judge, you will be judged; and with the measure you use, it will be measured back to you. And why do you look at the speck in your brother's eye, but do not consider the plank in your own eye? Or how can you say to your brother, 'Let me remove the speck from your eye'; and look, a plank is in your own eye? Hypocrite! First remove the plank from your own eye, and then you will see clearly to remove the speck from your brother's eye. Do not give what is holy to the dogs; nor cast your pearls before swine, lest they trample them under their feet, and turn and tear you in pieces."* (Matthew 7:1–6)

Judge or not judge — what is Jesus teaching here? The first question to answer is this: is the prohibition in verse 1 universal? The answer must be no. Notice verse 6: one has to discern who is a dog and who is a pig in order to obey Jesus. You cannot obey verse 6 unless you have made that categorization in your mind — that is a pig, that is a dog. You cannot obey it without making some sort of judgment.

Notice also that one must still help the brother with the speck in his eye, which means you need to discern that there is indeed a speck in the brother's eye — that there is indeed a sin in his life that should be addressed. So the prohibition against judging in verse 1 cannot be universal.

The question becomes: if not a universal prohibition, what is Jesus prohibiting? The core of what Jesus is forbidding is self-righteous judgment. In this passage, what qualifies a person to address another person's sin is the fact that he or she has judged himself or herself first — remove the plank from your own eye before you address the speck. The one qualified to go talk to a sinner is someone who is also a sinner — not someone who is self-righteous and can speak from a pedestal down to the lonely sinner.

A person who has honestly examined his own heart and life is able to come to the other person with humility, compassion, patience, kindness, and mercy — the fruit of the Spirit. That all comes from first judging yourself before going to the other person. This is key in conflict. When you have conflict in your life — and we all do — the key is to first look at yourself, and then go gently to the other person.

So Jesus is not universally forbidding judgment. In fact he is telling us to judge in this passage — but to judge with humility, not self-righteousness.

## Righteous and Unrighteous Judgment

There are other categories of unrighteous judgment beyond self-righteousness. One is jumping to conclusions — hearing only one side of a story and making a conclusion. Proverbs 18 says that the fool listens to one side and makes a conclusion before hearing the other. The Matthew 18 process of church discipline is itself a case of righteous judgment — going to a brother who has sinned, then bringing another, then bringing it to the church.

Another form of unrighteous judgment is judging by a standard other than the Word of God. In 1 Corinthians 4:3–7, Paul speaks about judgment with which he was wrongly judged, and he says it was unrighteous because he was *judged beyond what is written* — that is the expression he uses. To judge using some other standard than what is revealed to us in the Word of God is unrighteous judgment. Our confession says that the whole counsel of God — concerning everything required for his own glory, and for our salvation, faith, and life — is either expressly set down in Scripture or necessarily deduced from Scripture. So to judge by a different standard than the Word is unrighteous.

A third category of unrighteous judgment is judging matters of conscience. Romans 14 says that the Lord will judge every Christian, for each is a servant of God. If someone thinks, as a matter of conscience, that they should not do a certain thing, we should not despise them or force them to go against their conscience in matters where Scripture is not explicit. We can engage the brother in discussion, but we do not despise him and we do not coerce him.

A fourth category is judging the heart apart from observable fruit. That is making assumptions — judging a person's true intention without evidence. We are called to judge the fruit, not the heart. Jesus said: *"A tree is known by its fruit."* You know a person by what they do. When the church exercises discipline, we do not make a judgment about someone's heart. We say that they are not behaving or believing as a Christian ought, and therefore should not be treated as one. The Lord knows the heart. We are in charge of addressing observable behavior and belief.

## Test All Things by the Word of God

Where then are we to judge? All things, according to the Scriptures. Turn to 1 Thessalonians chapter 5.

> *"Test all things; hold fast what is good. Abstain from every form of evil."* (1 Thessalonians 5:21–22)

Test all things — prove all things, judge all things, compare all things to the Word of God. Grab hold of what is good and rid yourself of what is evil. What are some examples of *all things*?

We are to test **teaching and doctrine**. The Bible says the Bereans were more noble because they examined the Scriptures to see whether what Paul was saying was true. Timothy is told by Paul that those in Thessalonica were praised because they searched the Scriptures daily to find out whether these things were so. And Timothy himself is told to pay attention to doctrine.

We are to test **prophecy**. The verse just before, verse 20, says *do not despise prophecies* — test all things. When we think of prophecy, we tend to think of predicting the future. But less than two percent of all prophetic utterances in the Bible are about foretelling the future. Prophecy is ninety-eight percent about forthtelling — calling God's people back to God's Word. When Paul says do not despise prophecy, he means do not despise the forthtelling of God's Word. Test it. Keep what is good. Throw away what is evil.

John tells us to **test the spirits** — not disembodied things, but the spirit in which something is presented. Is it consistent with the Spirit of God or the spirit of Satan?

Paul tells us to **test leaders**. In 1 Timothy 3:10, speaking specifically of deacons — which we can extend to elders — he says: *let these also first be tested; then let them serve as deacons, being found blameless.* In 2 Corinthians 8:22, Paul writes: *we have sent with them our brother whom we have often proved diligent in many things.* The word *proved* there is the same word as *test*. We tested our brother and found him to be diligent.

We are also to **test and discern the times**. Jesus rebuked the Pharisees in Luke 12: *"Hypocrites! You can discern the face of the sky and of the earth, but how is it that you do not discern this time?"* You can tell from red skies whether rain is coming, but you cannot figure out that I am the Christ after all these signs and wonders. We need to discern the times in which we live.

And we are to **discern ourselves**. Paul says in 2 Corinthians 13: *Examine yourselves as to whether you are in the faith; test yourselves.* And in 1 Corinthians 11:28: *Let a man examine himself, and so let him eat of the bread and drink of the cup.*

## Conclusion: The Heart of Discernment Is the Bible

So we are called to judge — but to judge righteously, according to the Word of God. Not according to other standards, but according to what God has revealed.

You cannot be discerning without judging. In fact, you cannot be human and not judge. The way we think — we take one fact, we take another fact, we compare them, we make a judgment, and we create a new conclusion. Thesis, antithesis, synthesis — that is how we think. Our minds work by judging. The problem is not that we judge; the problem is that we judge unrighteously.

In order to be spiritually discerning, we need to judge righteously according to the Bible. That is the heart of discernment — the Bible, and what the Bible says about judging and judging righteously.

As for empathy in this context: we are not called to empathize with wickedness. We are called to empathize with suffering. When we are making a moral judgment about sin, we are not talking about suffering, we are talking about sin. But we can absolutely come to a brother who struggles with the same thing we have struggled with and say, I know what you are going through — let me help you through it. Empathize with the person, not with wickedness. And empathy does not have to be our first emotion or first reaction in every case.
